items.correct: 24 and 36 — 5 parts in total, each part is 12, so 2 x 12 = 24 and 3 x 12 = 36.
items.misconception: 30 and 30, by splitting 60 in half because there are two numbers in the ratio.
items.random_wrong: 20 and 30, by treating 2 : 3 as the actual amounts.
items.why: Students don't break the total into the correct number of equal parts, often defaulting to a 'fair' even split.

items.misconception: 8xy, by combining all terms regardless of variable.
items.random_wrong: 9xy, by adding all the numbers and merging the letters.
items.why: Students treat different variables as interchangeable, missing that only terms with the same variable (and power) can be combined.

items.q: If 5 pens cost £3.50, how much do 7 pens cost?
items.correct: £4.90 — find the unit cost (£0.70 per pen), then multiply by 7.
items.misconception: £5.50, by adding £2 to £3.50 because there are 2 more pens.
items.why: Students apply additive thinking (more pens means add more pounds) instead of multiplicative reasoning via the unit rate.
It's 2:3. “3:6” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's 2:3. “4:3” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Simplify 20:30 fully. Write the first number.
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
